coredata.dc:title:
Atomistic simulation of rare earth compounds Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo, Ni)
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Ping
Chen, Nan-Xian
Shen, Jiang
coredata.dc:description:
By using the interatomic pair potential obtained with the lattice inversion method, the structural stability of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo, Ni) of the rhombohedral Th2Zn17-type structure is studied. Calculated results show that adding Ti, V, Cr or Mo atoms makes the crystal cohesive energy of Sm2Fe17−
x T x decrease markedly, proving that these atoms can stabilize Sm2Fe17−
x T x structure. The calculated lattice constants coincide quite well with experimental values. The ternary element T preferentially substitutes for Co in the 6c site only. The calculated results also show that Ni does not stabilize the system with the structure of Th2Zn17-type. Moreover, the phonon densities of states are first evaluated for the Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo) compounds with the rhombohedral Th2Zn17-type structure. This may be an attempt to predict the thermodynamic properties for the rare earth materials with complex structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare earth compounds
Intermetallic compounds
Crystal structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title:
Vibrational properties of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
The dependence of cohesive energy on the content of T atoms in Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Cr, Ti, Mo, Ni).
Calculated phonon density of states for Sm2Fe15T2.
Calculated specific heat and vibrational entropy of Sm2Fe17 and Sm2Fe15T2 (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo).
Calculated Debye temperature of Sm2Fe17 and Sm2Fe15T2 (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo).
Determination of lattice constants of Sm2(Fe0.96Ti0.04)17 (the Ti atoms occupy the 6c site)

The calculated mean structural parameters and atomic positions of Sm2Fe17−
x Cr x with the experimental data [22] in brackets

article.ce:title:
Atomistic simulation of rare earth compounds Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo, Ni)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Physics, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
By using the interatomic pair potential obtained with the lattice inversion method, the structural stability of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo, Ni) of the rhombohedral Th2Zn17-type structure is studied. Calculated results show that adding Ti, V, Cr or Mo atoms makes the crystal cohesive energy of Sm2Fe17−
x T x decrease markedly, proving that these atoms can stabilize Sm2Fe17−
x T x structure. The calculated lattice constants coincide quite well with experimental values. The ternary element T preferentially substitutes for Co in the 6c site only. The calculated results also show that Ni does not stabilize the system with the structure of Th2Zn17-type. Moreover, the phonon densities of states are first evaluated for the Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo) compounds with the rhombohedral Th2Zn17-type structure. This may be an attempt to predict the thermodynamic properties for the rare earth materials with complex structures.

ce:section.ce:para:
Rare earth iron intermetallic compounds like R2Fe17 were found to have low Curie temperature T c and planar anisotropy [1,2]. However, the Sm2Fe17-based ternary or quaternary compounds have attracted a great deal of attention, because of their potential of being high performance permanent magnetic materials [3–5]. Ab inito calculations by Sabiryanov and Jaswal [6] indicated that the nitrogenation of Sm2Fe17 more than doubles its Curie temperature (T c) but lowers T c of Sm2Co17. They also calculated the exchange parameters (Jij ) of Sm2Fe16A (A = Ga, Si) by self-consistent spin-polarized LMTO, and simulated the Curie temperature using the Monte Carlo method [7]. Recently, Chen et al. have performed theoretical studies on the phase stability, site preference and lattice parameters for R(Fe, T)12 [8,9], Nd2Fe17−
x T x
[10,11] and R3Fe29−
x T x
[12], suggesting the validity of ab initio potentials on the structure simulation of such rare earth compounds. In the article, based on the ab initio potentials, the structural properties of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo, Ni) are studied. Also, the phonon densities of states for these compounds are evaluated, from which the Debye temperatures are obtained.

The inversion method was initially proposed by Carlsson et al. [13] to obtain the pair potential from the cohesive energy curves. The method was based on two assumptions, that the cohesive energy can be written as a sum of pair interaction energies and the electronic structure calculations of the cohesive energy are a function of volume. The expression was indigestible because infinite summations are included in it, each of them containing infinite terms. This made it inconvenient for analysis. In the mid-1990s, Chen developed a concise inversion method based on the modified Möbius inversion transform in number theory to avoid the above shortcomings [14–17]. Chen’s lattice inversion formula is a rigid and concise technique for obtaining parameter-free potentials, which can express the inversion coefficient of materials with identical structure concisely and uniformly, making it convenient to analyze them. And it has been applied successfully to study the site preference of ternary additions in Gd(Fe, T)12 [8], the lattice dynamics [18,19], the field-ion microscopy image of Fe3Al analysis [15] and so on.

In our previous works [8–12,15], some details of the method for obtaining the potentials are given. In the paper, we provide a brief description of the operational equations of Chen’s lattice inversion method. The cohesive energy E(x) for a crystal can be expressed as a sum of interatomic pair potentials, i.e.
(1)
E ( x ) =
1 2
∑
R i
≠ 0
Φ (
R i
)
=
1 2
∑
n = 1
∞
r ( n )
Φ [ b ( n ) x ]
where x is the nearest-neighbor interatomic distance, r(n) is the nth neighbor coordination number, b(n) represents the relative distance between the origin and the nth set of lattice points, Φ(x) is the pair potential function. Then, the general equation for pair potential Φ(x) can be expressed as
(2)
Φ ( x ) = 2
∑
n = 1
∞
I ( n ) E [ b ( n ) x ]
The coefficient I(n) can be obtained by
(3)
∑
b ( n ) | b ( k )
I ( n ) r
b
− 1
b ( k )
b ( n )
=
δ
k 1
I(n) is uniquely determined by the geometry of the crystal structure, not related to the concrete element category. Then the interatomic pair potentials can be obtained from the known cohesive energy function E(x). Some important relevant interatomic pair potential curves are shown in Fig. 1 , which are close to Morse function, that is
Φ ( x ) =
D 0
(
e
[ − γ ( x /
R 0
− 1 ) ]
− 2
e
[ − ( γ / 2 ) ( x /
R 0
− 1 ) ]
)
where D 0, R 0, and γ are the potential parameters.

In the calculation, we adopt the cohesive energy as a criterion to judge the structural stability and site preference. The average energy per atom of the substitution of ternary elements T for Fe atoms in Sm2Fe17 system with Th2Zn17-type structure is calculated using a series of quasi-ab initio interatomic potentials. Firstly, Fe atoms in each site are substituted upon T atoms with different concentrations. Then energy minimization process is applied to relax the ternary system under the interaction of the potentials. Thus, the average energy of final structure can be investigated and compared. Fig. 2 gives the cohesive energy of the Sm2Fe17−
x T x compounds as a function of the T content ‘x’. To avoid the accidental error, the results represent the arithmetic average for 20 stochastic samples. We notice that addition of the ternary element Ti, V, Cr or Mo to Sm2Fe17 as an atom replacing Fe leads to the crystal cohesive energy decrease markedly, illustrating that each of these elements can stabilize the crystal structure. The degree of the decrease in cohesive energy corresponds with the species and occupation sites of the ternary atoms. The cohesive energy is lower when the T atoms are substituted for Fe on the 6c sites rather than for Fe on the 18f and 18h sites. This indicates that the preferential site of T atoms is the 6c site, which agrees reasonably well with the experiment results [20–22]. Therefore, we concluded that the T atoms have the same site preference in Fe-based compounds as in the Co-based compounds [23], and the preferential sites are the 6c sites. But for T = Ni, the cohesive energy of Sm2Fe17−
x Ni x increase, which means that the Ni atoms cannot stabilize Sm2Fe17−
x Ni x with Th2Zn17 structure.

The substitution behaviors of ternary elements have been analyzed in detail from the point of view of interatomic pair potential in our previous work [10,11]. When small amount of ternary elements T substitute for Fe, the atoms surrounding each ternary atom are Fe atoms, the energy difference between structures before and after substitution is dominated by the difference between Φ Fe–T(r) and Φ Fe–Fe(r). If Φ Fe–T(r) < Φ Fe–Fe(r), T elements can stabilize the structure. On the contrary, if Φ Fe–T(r) > Φ Fe–Fe(r), then T elements cannot play the role of stabilization. This can explain why Ti, V, Cr, Mo play a role of stabilization, while Ni do not. When the amount of the ternary element increases, it becomes more likely for the T atoms to get close to each other and close to rare earth atoms. Then the comparison between Φ Sm–T(r) and Φ Sm–Fe(r), and between Φ Fe–Fe(r) and Φ T–T(r) have to be made. As an example, we choose T = Cr, one can see from Fig. 1 that Φ Sm–Cr(r) > Φ Sm–Fe(r) within the range of r < 3.4Å and Φ Cr–Cr(r) > Φ Fe–Fe(r) within the range of r < 2.7Å. This means that substitution of a large amount of the ternary element may cause the total energy to increase and leads to a structural instability. Hence, the solubility of the ternary element is not infinite.

To check the stability of the calculation structures, we allowed every atom in the Sm2(Fe0.96Ti0.04)17 crystal cell to randomly move in arbitrary directions. Then we used the conjugate gradient for energy minimization. The detail of lattice deformation is described in Table 1 . After the energy minimizations, the final relaxed structures show that our potentials exhibited good abilities for describing the stability of the Sm2Fe17−
x T x crystal, as shown in Table 1. Furthermore, in the process of minimization, the structure with each atom shifted 0.6Å from their equilibrium position can restore the same final structure as that undergoing global deformation. The calculated equilibrium lattice constants are consistent with the experimental data (a = 8.57Å, c = 12.50Å) [24]. With these pair potentials, we also calculated the equilibrium lattice constant, the atomic positions in a unit cell and volume of Sm2Fe17−
x T x . The results in Table 2 indicate that the calculations which represent the arithmetic average for 20 stochastic samples are consistent with the experimental observations [22].

In addition, the test could also be performed by using the same interatomic pair potentials in molecular dynamics simulations. The initial configuration was to put the Sm, Fe atoms on the equilibrium sites and the Cr atoms on the 6c sites of Sm2Fe17−
x Cr x
, corresponding to the lowest energy. The constant-NPT molecular dynamics [25] is applied using the method of Berendsen et al. [26]. The MD simulations were carried out for (Sm2Fe16Cr1)27 of 1539 atoms in a rhombohedral cell from 100 to 900K at a step of 200K. For each temperature at 1atm pressure, the calculations were performed for 30,000 time steps, where one time step was 2.0 × 10−15 s. After reaching equilibrium, the crystal symmetry could also remain
R
3 ¯
m
space group. The calculated lattice constants of Sm2Fe16Cr1 are a = 8.526Å, c = 12.539Å at 300K and a = 8.529Å, c = 12.541Å at 500K (we choose T = 300 and 500K as the example), respectively, which change very little with respect to temperature variation. These results further verify that the above calculations are self-consistent and reasonable.

So, it can be estimated from the above calculations that the site preference and lattice parameters of Sm2Fe17−
x T x evaluated using the quasi-ab initio interatomic potentials agree well with experiment, which demonstrates that the potentials are reliable.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Vibrational properties of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo)
ce:section.ce:para:
Phonon density of states (DOS) reflects the lattice dynamic properties, and the derived specific heat and vibrational entropy are important thermodynamic parameters. Starting with the effective potentials and lattice dynamic theory, we calculate the total phonon DOS and the partial DOS of different elements for Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo) in a crystal cell including 57 atoms. The calculated DOS, g(ω), in a cell is shown in Fig. 3 . It can be observed from Fig. 3, that the highest frequency of Sm2Fe15Ti2 is 10.06THz and high-frequency localized modes appear above 8.29THz. The vibrational modes are mainly contributed with the Fe atoms in the total frequency range, and the Sm atoms have a little contribution to the lower frequency modes. As shown in Fig. 1, the very strong interaction between Co and Co at their nearest distance (2.441Å) causes Co to vibrate in the whole frequency range. The distance between Sm and its nearest Fe atoms is 3.012Å, so the Sm atoms only contribute to the lower frequency vibrations because of the infirm interaction in this range and the large atomic mass of Sm. When the ternary elements T are substituted for Fe atoms at the 6c sites in Sm2Fe17, the changes of the curves and of the contribution of Fe atoms to the modes are not obvious. When the frequency is less than 8THz, the shapes of the frequency modes contributed by the ternary elements are similar to those contributed by Fe atoms, but the amplitude is much smaller. This is mainly because the T atoms randomly substitute for Fe in the 6c site. There is one nearest Fe neighbor at the 6c site, and there are six Fe neighbors at the 18f site, three at the 18f site and three at the 9d site in the Sm2Fe17−
x T x compounds. The distances between the T atom at 6c and these atoms are 2.441Å to the single 6c site, 2.793Å to the six 18f sites, 2.706Å to the three 18h sites and 2.598Å to the three 9d sites. So considering the nearest neighbor interaction alone, the nearest neighbor distances at the occupied 6c site will be between 2.598 and 2.793Å. From Fig. 1, we can infer that at a distance of about 2.7Å, the Fe–Fe interaction is nearly the Fe–T interaction. Therefore, the frequency modes of the ternary elements are almost the same with Fe in shape, but the amplitude is much smaller because the content of ternary elements is little.

Based on the calculation DOS, the lattice specific heat, C v(T), and vibrational entropy, S(T) can be obtained by
C v
( T ) = 3 N
k B
∫ 0 ∞
( ℏ ω /
k B
T )
2
e
ℏ ω /
k B
T
(
e
ℏ ω /
k B
T
− 1 )
2
g ( ω )
d ω
and
S ( T ) =
∫ 0 T
C v
(
T ′
)
T ′
d
T ′
Fig. 4 shows the calculated values of lattice specific heat at constant volume and vibrational entropy as a function of temperature, respectively. The numerical values of C v(T) and S(T) for Sm2Fe17−
x T x compound were calculated for the desired temperature range from 0 to 500K. The variation of the specific heat and entropy with temperature for the ternary compounds does not show any anomaly and the features are the same as in the Sm2Fe17 binary compound.

In Fig. 5 , we have reported the calculated values of the Debye temperature as a function of temperature. The Debye temperature of a material is a suitable parameter to describe phenomena of solid-state physics that are associated with lattice vibrations. Fig. 5 clearly shows that the values of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo) are 458.36, 376.21, 392.61, and 465.97K near 0K, respectively, that is, the values of the Debye temperature are larger for Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = V, Cr) than that for Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, Mo). As a last point, due to the absence of experimental data for the specific heat, we could not compare our results with the calculations.

The ab initio interatomic potentials obtained through the lattice inversion method are successfully used for the calculation of phase stability of Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo, Ni), site preference of ternary elements T in Sm2Fe17−
x T x and related parameters. The calculated results are in good agreement with the experimental data and could be well explained with pair potentials. Besides, the energy minimization method is used to relax the structure with global deformation and with random shift of all atoms. The stability of the structure is further confirmed through molecular dynamics at different temperatures. The calculated distorted system can restore the same final structure with the rhombohedral Th2Zn17-type. These facts verify that the interatomic pair potentials based on the lattice inversion method can effectively give a deeper insight into the structure and property of complex materials. Finally, based on the same inverted potentials, the thermodynamic properties for Sm2Fe17−
x T x (T = Ti, V, Cr, Mo) compounds are investigated.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the stimulating discussions with Professor F.M. Yang and G.M. Zhang. The present work was supported by the National 973 Project TG2000067101 and TG2000067106.
